sections.heading: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund
sections.summary: Section 1 identifies the entities to which these guidelines apply, including competent authorities, alternative investment fund managers, UCITS, investment companies, central counterparties, trade repositories, investment firms, credit institutions, central securities depositories, credit rating agencies, securitisation repositories, and administrators of critical benchmarks.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) and depositaries of alternative investment funds (AIFs), (ii) undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS), management companies and depositaries of UCITS, and investment companies that have not designated a management company authorised pursuant to UCITS Directive (iii) central counterparties (CCPs), including Tier 2 third-country CCPs which comply with the relevant EMIR requirements, (iv) trade repositories (TRs), (v) investment firms and credit institutions when carrying out investment services and activities, data reporting services providers and market operators of trading venues, (vi) central securities depositories (CSDs), (vii) credit rating agencies (CRAs), (viii) securitisation repositories (SRs), and (ix) administrators of critical benchmarks.

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) and depositaries of alternative investment funds (AIFs), (ii) undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS), management companies and depositaries of UCITS, and investment companies that have not designated a management company authorised pursuant to UCITS Directive (iii) central counterparties (CCPs), including Tier 2 third-country CCPs which comply with the relevant EMIR requirements, (iv) trade repositories (TRs), (v) investment firms and credit institutions when carrying out investment services and activities, data reporting services providers and market operators of trading venues, (vi) central securities depositories (CSDs), (vii) credit rating agencies (CRAs), (viii) securitisation repositories (SRs), and (ix) administrators of critical benchmarks.

sections.heading: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which
sections.summary: Section 2 states that ESMA will consider the compliance of Tier 2 third-country CCPs with comparable requirements in their home country when assessing whether they meet EMIR requirements.

requirements.text: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which compliance with the relevant EMIR requirements by a Tier 2 third-country CCP is satisfied by its compliance with comparable requirements in the third country pursuant to Article 25(2b)(a) of EMIR.

sections.source_quote: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which compliance with the relevant EMIR requirements by a Tier 2 third-country CCP is satisfied by its compliance with comparable requirements in the third country pursuant to Article 25(2b)(a) of EMIR.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions:
sections.summary: Section 3 lists the specific provisions of various regulations and directives to which these guidelines apply, including AIFMD, UCITS Directive, EMIR, SFTR, MiFID II, CSDR, CRA Regulation, SECR, Benchmarks Regulation, and others.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions: Articles 15, 18, 20 and 21(8) of AIFMD; Articles 13, 22, 38, 39, 40, 44, 45, 57(1)(d), 57(2), 57(3), 58, 75, 76, 77, 79, 81, 82 and 98 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2013/231; Articles 12(1)(a), 13, 14(1)(c), 22, 22a, 23(2), 30 and 31 of UCITS Directive; Article 4(1) to 4(3), 4(5), 5(2), 7, 9, 23(4), 32, 38, 39 and 40 of Commission Directive 2010/43/EU; Articles 2(2)(j), 3(1), 13(2), 15, 16 and 22 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2016/438; Articles 25, 26(1), 26(3), 26(6), 34, 35 and 78-81 of EMIR; Articles 5 and 12 of SFTR; Articles 3(1)(f), 3(2), 4, 7(2)(d) and (f), 9 and 17 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 153/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 150/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/359; Articles 16(2), 16(4), 16(5), 18(1), 19(3)(a), 47(1)(b) and (c), 48(1), 64(4), 65(5) and 66(3)1 of MiFID II; Articles 21(1) to (3), 23, 29(5), 30, 31 and 32 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/565; Articles 6, 15 and 16 (6) of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/584; Articles 6, 7, 8 and 9 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/571; Articles 22, 26, 30, 42, 44 and 45 of CSDR and Articles 33, 47, 50 (1), 57(2)(i), 66, 68, 75, 76, 78 and 80 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/392; Article 9 and Annex I, Section A points 4 and 8 and Annex II point 17 of CRA Regulation and Articles 11 and 25 of the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2012/449; Article 10(2) of SECR; Articles 6(3) and 10 of the Benchmarks Regulation and Point 7 of Annex I of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/1646.

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions: Articles 15, 18, 20 and 21(8) of AIFMD; Articles 13, 22, 38, 39, 40, 44, 45, 57(1)(d), 57(2), 57(3), 58, 75, 76, 77, 79, 81, 82 and 98 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2013/231; Articles 12(1)(a), 13, 14(1)(c), 22, 22a, 23(2), 30 and 31 of UCITS Directive; Article 4(1) to 4(3), 4(5), 5(2), 7, 9, 23(4), 32, 38, 39 and 40 of Commission Directive 2010/43/EU; Articles 2(2)(j), 3(1), 13(2), 15, 16 and 22 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2016/438; Articles 25, 26(1), 26(3), 26(6), 34, 35 and 78-81 of EMIR; Articles 5 and 12 of SFTR; Articles 3(1)(f), 3(2), 4, 7(2)(d) and (f), 9 and 17 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 153/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 150/2013; Articles 16 and 21 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/359; Articles 16(2), 16(4), 16(5), 18(1), 19(3)(a), 47(1)(b) and (c), 48(1), 64(4), 65(5) and 66(3)1 of MiFID II; Articles 21(1) to (3), 23, 29(5), 30, 31 and 32 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/565; Articles 6, 15 and 16 (6) of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/584; Articles 6, 7, 8 and 9 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/571; Articles 22, 26, 30, 42, 44 and 45 of CSDR and Articles 33, 47, 50 (1), 57(2)(i), 66, 68, 75, 76, 78 and 80 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2017/392; Article 9 and Annex I, Section A points 4 and 8 and Annex II point 17 of CRA Regulation and Articles 11 and 25 of the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2012/449; Article 10(2) of SECR; Articles 6(3) and 10 of the Benchmarks Regulation and Point 7 of Annex I of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/1646.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Section 4 of these guidelines states that they apply to all cloud outsourcing arrangements entered into, renewed, or amended on or after 31 July 2021. Firms are required to review and amend existing arrangements by 31 December 2022 to ensure compliance. If the review is not completed by this date, firms must inform their competent authority of the delay and provide details of their planned measures or exit strategy. Section 4 establishes that these guidelines apply to all cloud outsourcing arrangements from 31 July 2021. It defines the scope of entities subject to the guidelines, including investment firms, credit institutions, data reporting services providers, market operators, and others as specified in MiFID II and related regulations. The section also outlines various cloud deployment models.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it outlines the timeline and obligations for firms to review and update their cloud outsourcing arrangements, which may impact data transfer and processing across borders. This section is relevant to DTIA as it defines the scope of entities subject to cloud outsourcing guidelines and outlines different cloud deployment models that may impact data transfer and processing arrangements.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply to all cloud outsourcing arrangements entered into, renewed, or amended on or after 31 July 2021.
Firms should review and amend existing cloud outsourcing arrangements to ensure they take into account these guidelines by 31 December 2022.

If the review of cloud outsourcing arrangements of critical or important functions is not finalised by 31 December 2022, firms should inform their competent authority of this fact, including the measures planned to complete the review or the possible exit strategy.
These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements.

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements entered into, renewed or amended on or after this date.

sections.heading: These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives
sections.summary: Section 5 states that these guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation and aim to establish consistent supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision (ESFS) and ensure uniform application of the requirements in Section 1.1 regarding cloud outsourcing.

requirements.text: These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives of these guidelines are to establish consistent, efficient and effective supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision (ESFS) and to ensure the common, uniform and consistent application of the requirements referred to in Section 1.1 under the heading ‘What?' where firms outsource to CSPs.

sections.source_quote: These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives of these guidelines are to establish consistent, efficient and effective supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision (ESFS) and to ensure the common, uniform and consistent application of the requirements referred to in Section 1.1 under the heading ‘What?' where firms outsource to CSPs.

sections.heading: Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating
sections.summary: Section 7 requires competent authorities to incorporate these guidelines into their national legal and/or supervisory frameworks, ensuring that firms comply with the guidelines through supervision.

requirements.text: Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating them into their national legal and/or supervisory frameworks as appropriate, including where particular guidelines are directed primarily at firms. In this case, competent authorities should ensure, through their supervision, that firms comply with the guidelines.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating them into their national legal and/or supervisory frameworks as appropriate, including where particular guidelines are directed primarily at firms. In this case, competent authorities should ensure, through their supervision, that firms comply with the guidelines.

sections.heading: Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these
sections.summary: Section 8 states that ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by CRAs, TRs, SRs, Tier 2 third-country CCPs, and data reporting services providers and administrators of EU critical benchmarks from 1 January 2022.

requirements.text: Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by CRAs, TRs, SRs, Tier 2 third-country CCPs and, from 1 January 2022, data reporting services providers and administrators of EU critical benchmarks.

sections.source_quote: Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by CRAs, TRs, SRs, Tier 2 third-country CCPs and, from 1 January 2022, data reporting services providers and administrators of EU critical benchmarks.

sections.heading: Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all
sections.summary: Section 9 requires competent authorities to notify ESMA within two months of the publication of the guidelines in all EU official languages whether they comply, intend to comply, or do not intend to comply with the guidelines.

requirements.text: Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages, competent authorities to which these guidelines apply must notify ESMA whether they (i) comply, (ii) do not comply, but intend to comply, or (iii) do not comply and do not intend to comply with the guidelines.

sections.source_quote: Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages, competent authorities to which these guidelines apply must notify ESMA whether they (i) comply, (ii) do not comply, but intend to comply, or (iii) do not comply and do not intend to comply with the guidelines.

sections.heading: In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two
sections.summary: Section 10 requires competent authorities to notify ESMA within two months of the publication of the guidelines in all EU official languages of their reasons for non-compliance.

requirements.text: In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages of their reasons for not complying with the guidelines. A template for notifications is available on ESMA's website.

sections.source_quote: In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages of their reasons for not complying with the guidelines. A template for notifications is available on ESMA's website.

sections.heading: Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.
sections.summary: Section 11 states that firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.

requirements.text: Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.

sections.source_quote: Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.

sections.heading: A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is
sections.summary: Section 12 requires firms to have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is consistent with their internal policies and processes, including information security and operational risk management.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to have a cloud outsourcing strategy that aligns with internal policies and includes information security and operational risk management, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is consistent with the firm's relevant strategies and internal policies and processes, including in relation to information and communication technology, information security, and operational risk management.

sections.source_quote: A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is consistent with the firm's relevant strategies and internal policies and processes, including in relation to information and communication technology, information security, and operational risk management.

sections.heading: A firm should:
sections.summary: Section 13 requires firms to assign responsibilities for cloud outsourcing documentation, management, and control, allocate resources for compliance, and establish an oversight function for managing cloud outsourcing risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to assign responsibilities, allocate resources, and establish oversight functions for managing cloud outsourcing risks, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: A firm should clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements within its organisation;
A firm should allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with these guidelines and all of the legal requirements applicable to its cloud outsourcing arrangements;

A firm should establish a cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff members who are directly accountable to the management body and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements.

sections.source_quote: A firm should clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements within its organisation; A firm should allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with these guidelines and all of the legal requirements applicable to its cloud outsourcing arrangements; A firm should establish a cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff members who are directly accountable to the management body and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements.

sections.heading: A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the
sections.summary: Section 14 requires firms to monitor the performance, security measures, and adherence to service levels by their CSPs, with a risk-based focus on critical or important functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to monitor CSP performance, security, and service levels, with a focus on critical functions, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the adherence to agreed service levels by its CSPs. This monitoring should be risk-based, with a primary focus on the critical or important functions that have been outsourced.

sections.source_quote: A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the adherence to agreed service levels by its CSPs. This monitoring should be risk-based, with a primary focus on the critical or important functions that have been outsourced.

sections.heading: A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical
sections.summary: Section 15 requires firms to periodically reassess whether their cloud outsourcing arrangements concern critical or important functions, especially when there is a material change in risk, nature, or scale.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to reassess cloud outsourcing arrangements for critical functions when there is a material change, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical or important function periodically and whenever the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed.

sections.source_quote: A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical or important function periodically and whenever the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed.

sections.heading: A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: Section 16 requires firms to maintain an updated register of cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between critical and non-critical functions, and to maintain records of terminated arrangements for an appropriate period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to maintain an updated register of cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between critical and non-critical functions, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements.
Taking into account national law, a firm should also maintain a record of terminated cloud outsourcing arrangements for an appropriate time period.

sections.source_quote: A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements. Taking into account national law, a firm should also maintain a record of terminated cloud outsourcing arrangements for an appropriate time period.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the
sections.summary: Section 17 requires firms to include specific information in the register for cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, including details about the CSP, data storage locations, and risk assessments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to include detailed information in the register for critical cloud outsourcing arrangements, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the register should include at least the following information for each cloud outsourcing arrangement: a reference number; the start date and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; a brief description of the outsourced function, including the data that is outsourced and whether this data includes personal data; a category assigned by the firm that reflects the nature of the outsourced function; whether the outsourced function supports business operations that are time-critical; the name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier (where available), its registered address, its relevant contact details and the name of its parent company (if any); the governing law of the cloud outsourcing arrangement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; the type of cloud services and deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held and the locations (namely regions or countries) where such data may be stored; the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function and the date of the next planned assessment; the date of the most recent risk assessment/audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment/audit; the individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement; where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which a critical or important function (or material parts thereof) is sub-outsourced, including the countries where the sub-outsourcers are registered, where the sub-outsourced service will be performed, and the locations (namely regions or countries) where the data will be stored; the estimated annual budget cost of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.source_quote: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the register should include at least the following information for each cloud outsourcing arrangement: a reference number; the start date and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; a brief description of the outsourced function, including the data that is outsourced and whether this data includes personal data; a category assigned by the firm that reflects the nature of the outsourced function; whether the outs, outsourced function supports business operations that are time-critical; the name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier (where available), its registered address, its relevant contact details and the name of its parent company (if any); the governing law of the cloud outsourcing arrangement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; the type of cloud services and deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held and the locations (namely regions or countries) where such data may be stored; the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function and the date of the next planned assessment; the date of the most recent risk assessment/audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment/audit; the individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement; where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which a critical or important function (or material parts thereof) is sub-outsourced, including the countries where the sub-outsourcers are registered, where the sub-outsourced service will be performed, and the locations (namely regions or countries) where the data will be stored; the estimated annual budget cost of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important
sections.summary: Section 18 requires firms to define the information to be included in the register for non-critical or non-important functions based on the nature, scale, and complexity of the risks involved.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to define the information to be included in the register for non-critical cloud outsourcing arrangements, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important functions, a firm should define the information to be included in the register based on the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to the outsourced function.

sections.source_quote: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important functions, a firm should define the information to be included in the register based on the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to the outsourced function.

sections.heading: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should:
sections.summary: Section 19 requires firms to assess whether a cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function, identify and assess risks, undertake due diligence on the CSP, and identify and assess conflicts of interest.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to assess the criticality of cloud outsourcing arrangements, identify risks, and conduct due diligence on CSPs, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should: assess if the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function; identify and assess all relevant risks of the cloud outsourcing arrangement; undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective CSP; identify and assess any conflict of interest that the outsourcing may cause.

sections.source_quote: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should: assess if the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function; identify and assess all relevant risks of the cloud outsourcing arrangement; undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective CSP; identify and assess any conflict of interest that the outsourcing may cause.

sections.heading: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be
sections.summary: Section 20 requires firms to conduct pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP, proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of the function and the risks involved.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to conduct proportionate pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on CSPs, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm intends to outsource and the risks inherent to this function. It should include at least an assessment of the potential impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm's operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks.

sections.source_quote: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm intends to outsource and the risks inherent to this function. It should include at least an assessment of the potential impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm's operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks.

sections.heading: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a
sections.summary: Section 21 requires firms to assess all relevant risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements, including information security, business continuity, legal compliance, reputational risks, and possible oversight limitations, when outsourcing critical or important functions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to assess a wide range of risks associated with cloud outsourcing of critical functions, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a firm should also: assess all relevant risks that may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including risks in relation to information and communication technology, information security, business continuity, legal and compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and possible oversight limitations for the firm, arising from: the selected cloud service and the proposed deployment models; the migration and/or the implementation processes; the sensitivity of the function and the related data which are under consideration to be outsourced and the security measures which would need to be taken; the interoperability of the systems and applications of the firm and the CSP, namely their capacity to exchange information and mutually use the information that has been exchanged; the portability of the data of the firm, namely the capacity to easily transfer the firm's data from one CSP to another or back to the firm; the political stability, the security situation and the legal system (including the law enforcement provisions in place, the insolvency law provisions that would apply in case of the CSP's bankruptcy, the laws on data protection in force and whether the conditions for transfer of personal data to a third country under the GDPR are met) of the countries (within or outside the EU) where the outsourced functions would be provided and where the outsourced data would be stored; in case of sub-outsourcing, the additional risks that may arise if the sub-outsourcer is located in a third country or a different country from the CSP and, in case of a sub-outsourcing chain, any additional risk which may arise, including in relation to the absence of a direct contract between the firm and the sub-outsourcer performing the outsourced function; possible concentration within the firm (including, where applicable, at the level of its group,) caused by multiple cloud outsourcing arrangements with the same CSP as well as possible concentration within the EU financial sector, caused by multiple firms making use of the same CSP or a small group of CSPs. When assessing the concentration risk, the firm should take into account all its cloud outsourcing arrangements (and, where applicable, the cloud outsourcing arrangements at the level of its group) with that CSP;

take into account the expected benefits and costs of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including weighing any significant risks which may be reduced or better managed against any significant risks which may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.source_quote: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a firm should also: assess all relevant risks that may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including risks in relation to information and communication technology, information security, business continuity, legal and compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and possible oversight limitations for the firm, arising from: the selected cloud service and the proposed deployment models; the migration and/or the implementation processes; the sensitivity of the function and the related data which are under consideration to be outsourced and the security measures which would need to be taken; the interoperability of the systems and applications of the firm and the CSP, namely their capacity to exchange information and mutually use the information that has been exchanged; the portability of the data of the firm, namely the capacity to easily transfer the firm's data from one CSP to another or back to the firm; the political stability, the security situation and the legal system (including the law enforcement provisions in place, the insolvency law provisions that would apply in case of the CSP's bankruptcy, the laws on data protection in force and whether the conditions for transfer of personal data to a third country under the GDPR are met) of the countries (within or outside the EU) where the outsourced functions would be provided and where the outsourced data would be stored; in case of sub-outsourcing, the additional risks that may arise if the sub-outsourcer is located in a third country or a different country from the CSP and, in case of a sub-outsourcing chain, any additional risk which may arise, including in relation to the absence of a direct contract between the firm and the sub-outsourcer performing the outsourced function; possible concentration within the firm (including, where applicable, at the level of its group,) caused by multiple cloud outsourcing arrangements with the same CSP as well as possible concentration within the EU financial sector, caused by multiple firms making use of the same CSP or a small group of CSPs. When assessing the concentration risk, the firm should take into account all its cloud outsourcing arrangements (and, where applicable, the cloud outsourcing arrangements at the level of its group) with that CSP; take into account the expected benefits and costs of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including weighing any significant risks which may be reduced or better managed against any significant risks which may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include
sections.summary: Section 22 requires firms to evaluate the suitability of the CSP, including its business reputation, skills, resources, organisational structure, and relevant authorisations or registrations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates firms to evaluate the suitability of CSPs, including their business reputation, skills, and authorisations, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include an evaluation of the suitability of the CSP. When assessing the suitability of the CSP, a firm should ensure that the CSP has the business reputation, the skills, the resources (including human, IT and financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the relevant authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner and to meet its obligations over the duration of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.source_quote: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include an evaluation of the suitability of the CSP. When assessing the suitability of the CSP, a firm should ensure that the CSP has the business reputation, the skills, the resources (including human, IT and financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the relevant authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner and to meet its obligations over the duration of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also
sections.summary: Section 23 allows firms to use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports to support due diligence on CSPs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows firms to use certifications and audit reports to support due diligence on CSPs, which is relevant for assessing data transfer and storage risks.

requirements.text: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports.

sections.source_quote: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports.

sections.heading: If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the
sections.summary: This section requires firms to promptly review or re-perform due diligence on a CSP if there are significant deficiencies or changes to the services provided or the CSP's situation. It emphasizes the need for ongoing assessment of the CSP's reliability and performance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates continuous review of CSPs, ensuring that data transfer and processing arrangements remain compliant with evolving risks and conditions.

requirements.text: Firms must promptly review or re-perform due diligence on a CSP if there are significant deficiencies or changes to the services provided or the CSP's situation.

sections.source_quote: 24. If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the services provided or to the situation of the CSP, the pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the CSP should be promptly reviewed or where needed re-performed.

sections.heading: In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with
sections.summary: This section requires firms to assess, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed when entering into a new arrangement or renewing an existing one with a CSP that has already been assessed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates a risk-based assessment of CSPs, ensuring that data transfer and processing arrangements are continuously evaluated for compliance and risk.

requirements.text: Firms must assess, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed when entering into a new arrangement or renewing an existing one with a CSP that has already been assessed.

sections.source_quote: 25. In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with a CSP that has already been assessed, it should determine, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should
sections.summary: This section outlines the minimum contractual requirements for cloud outsourcing agreements, including clear descriptions of outsourced functions, data locations, security provisions, monitoring rights, service levels, incident reporting, and access rights.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is highly relevant to DTIA as it mandates detailed contractual safeguards for cross-border data transfers and processing, including data location, security, and access rights.

requirements.text: Written agreements must include a clear description of the outsourced function.
Written agreements must specify the start and end dates of the agreement and notice periods for both the firm and CSP.
Written agreements must include the governing law and choice of jurisdiction.
Written agreements must outline the financial obligations of both the firm and CSP.
Written agreements must specify whether sub-outsourcing is permitted and the conditions for it.
Written agreements must specify the locations where the outsourced function will be provided and where data will be processed and stored, including a requirement to notify the firm of any changes.
Written agreements must include provisions regarding information security and protection of personal data.
Written agreements must allow the firm to monitor the CSP's performance regularly.
Written agreements must include agreed service levels with quantitative and qualitative performance targets.
Written agreements must include reporting obligations of the CSP to the firm and relevant security functions.
Written agreements must include provisions regarding the management of incidents by the CSP, including the obligation to report incidents without undue delay.
Written agreements must specify whether the CSP should take mandatory insurance against certain risks and the level of insurance cover requested.
Written agreements must require the CSP to implement and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans.
Written agreements must grant the firm, its competent authorities, and appointed persons access and audit rights to monitor the CSP's performance and compliance.
Written agreements must ensure that data processed or stored by the CSP can be accessed, recovered, and returned to the firm as needed.

sections.source_quote: 28. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should include at least: a) a clear description of the outsourced function; b) the start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and the notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) the governing law of the agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; d) the firm's and the CSP's financial obligations; e) whether sub-outsourcing is permitted, and, if so, under which conditions, having regard to Guideline 7; f) the location(s) (namely regions or countries) where the outsourced function will be provided and where data will be processed and stored, and the conditions to be met, including a requirement to notify the firm if the CSP proposes to change the location(s); g) provisions regarding information security and protection of personal data, having regard to Guideline 4; h) the right for the firm to monitor the CSP's performance under the cloud outsourcing arrangement on a regular basis, having regard to Guideline 6; i) the agreed service levels, which should include, quantitative and qualitative performance targets in order to allow for timely monitoring so that appropriate corrective actions can be taken without undue delay if agreed service levels are not met; j) the reporting obligations of the CSP to the firm and, as appropriate, the obligations to submit reports relevant for the firm's security function and key functions, such as reports prepared by the internal audit function of the CSP; k) provisions regarding the management of incidents by the CSP, including the obligation for the CSP to report to the firm without undue delay incidents that have affected the operation of the firm's contracted service; l) whether the CSP should take mandatory insurance against certain risks and, if applicable, the level of insurance cover requested; m) the requirements for the CSP to implement and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans; n) the requirement for the CSP to grant the firm, its competent authorities and any other person appointed by the firm or the competent authorities the right to access and to inspect the relevant information, premises, systems and devices of the CSP to the extent necessary to monitor the CSP's performance under the cloud outsourcing arrangement and its compliance with the applicable regulatory and contractual requirements, having regard to Guideline 6; o) provisions to ensure that the data that the CSP processes or stores on behalf of the firm can be accessed, recovered and returned to the firm as needed, having regard to Guideline 5.

sections.heading: A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and
sections.summary: This section requires firms to set and monitor information security requirements in internal policies and cloud outsourcing agreements, ensuring the protection of confidential, personal, and sensitive data in proportion to the function's nature, scale, and complexity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates the implementation of information security requirements to protect data processed or stored by CSPs, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must set information security requirements in internal policies and cloud outsourcing agreements and monitor compliance on an ongoing basis.
Firms must ensure that these requirements are proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of the outsourced function and the inherent risks.

sections.source_quote: 29. A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and procedures and within the cloud outsourcing written agreement and monitor compliance with these requirements on an ongoing basis, including to protect confidential, personal or otherwise sensitive data. These requirements should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm outsources to the CSP and the risks inherent to this function.

sections.heading: For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without
sections.summary: This section requires firms to apply a risk-based approach to information security when outsourcing critical or important functions, ensuring clear roles, strong authentication, encryption, network security, API security, business continuity, data location, and compliance with information security standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is highly relevant to DTIA as it mandates detailed information security requirements for cross-border data transfers and processing, including encryption, access controls, and compliance with international standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure a clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities between the firm and CSP, including threat detection, incident management, and patch management.
Firms must ensure strong authentication mechanisms and access controls to prevent unauthorized access to data and cloud resources.
Firms must ensure relevant encryption technologies are used for data in transit, memory, at rest, and backups, with appropriate key management solutions.
Firms must consider appropriate levels of network availability, segregation, and processing environments to ensure security.
Firms must consider mechanisms for secure API integration to prevent unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction of data.
Firms must ensure effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls, including geographically spread hosting options and data replication.
Firms must adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and processing locations.
Firms must verify that the CSP complies with internationally recognized information security standards and has implemented appropriate controls.

sections.source_quote: 30. For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without prejudice to the applicable requirements under GDPR, a firm, applying a risk-based approach, should at least: a) information security organisation: ensure that there is a clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities between the firm and the CSP, including in relation to threat detection, incident management and patch management, and ensure that the CSP is effectively able to fulfil its roles and responsibilities; b) identity and access management: ensure that strong authentication mechanisms (for example multi-factor authentication) and access controls are in place with a view to prevent unauthorised access to the firm's data and back-end cloud resources; c) encryption and key management: ensure that relevant encryption technologies are used, where necessary, for data in transit, data in memory, data at rest and data back-ups, in combination with appropriate key management solutions to limit the risk of non-authorised access to the encryption keys; in particular, the firm should consider state-of-the-art technology and processes when selecting its key management solution; d) operations and network security: consider appropriate levels of network availability, network segregation (for example tenant isolation in the shared environment of the cloud, operational separation as regards the web, application logic, operating system, network, Data Base Management System (DBMS) and storage layers) and processing environments (for example test, User Acceptance Testing, development, production); e) application programming interfaces (API): consider mechanisms for the integration of the cloud services with the systems of the firm to ensure security of APIs (for example establishing and maintaining information security policies and procedures for APIs across multiple system interfaces, jurisdictions, and business functions to prevent unauthorised disclosure, modification or destruction of data); f) business continuity and disaster recovery: ensure that effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls are in place (for example by setting minimum capacity requirements, selecting hosting options that are geographically spread, with the capability to switch from one to the other, or requesting and reviewing documentation showing the transport route of the firm's data among the CSP's systems, as well as considering the possibility to replicate machine images to an independent storage location, which is sufficiently isolated from the network or taken offline); g) data location: adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s) (namely regions or countries); h) compliance & monitoring: verify that the CSP complies with internationally recognised information security standards and has implemented appropriate information security controls (for example by requesting the CSP to provide evidence that it conducts relevant information security reviews and by performing regular assessments and tests on the CSP's information security arrangements).

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is
sections.summary: This section requires firms to ensure they can exit cloud outsourcing arrangements without undue disruption to business activities, services, or compliance, and without compromising data confidentiality, integrity, or availability. It mandates the development of comprehensive exit plans and transition strategies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates the development of exit strategies to ensure data can be recovered and transferred without disruption, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure they can exit cloud outsourcing arrangements without undue disruption to business activities, services, or compliance, and without compromising data confidentiality, integrity, or availability.
Firms must develop comprehensive, documented, and tested exit plans that are updated as needed.
Firms must identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans to remove the outsourced function and data from the CSP and transfer them to an alternative CSP or back to the firm.

Firms must ensure the cloud outsourcing agreement includes an obligation for the CSP to support the orderly transfer of the outsourced function and related data to another CSP or directly to the firm.
Firms must ensure the CSP agrees to securely delete data from its systems and any sub-outsourcer's systems.

sections.source_quote: 31. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is able to exit the cloud outsourcing arrangement without undue disruption to its business activities and services to its clients, and without any detriment to its compliance with its obligations under the applicable legislation, as well as the confidentiality, integrity and availability of its data. For that purpose, a firm should: a) develop exit plans that are comprehensive, documented and sufficiently tested. These plans should be updated as needed, including in case of changes in the outsourced function; b) identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans to remove the outsourced function and data from the CSP and, where applicable, any sub-outsourcer, and transfer them to the alternative CSP indicated by the firm or directly back to the firm. These solutions should be defined with regard to the challenges that may arise from the location of the data, taking the necessary measures to ensure business continuity during the transition phase; c) ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement includes an obligation for the CSP to support the orderly transfer of the outsourced function, and the related processing of data, from the CSP and any sub-outsourcer to another CSP indicated by the firm or directly to the firm in case the firm activates the exit strategy. The obligation to support the orderly transfer of the outsourced function, and the related treatment of data, should include where relevant the secure deletion of the data from the systems of the CSP and any sub-outsourcer.

sections.heading: When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above
sections.summary: This section requires firms to consider various factors when developing exit strategies, including defining objectives, trigger events, conducting business impact analyses, assigning roles, testing the strategy, and defining success criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates the development of comprehensive exit strategies, including testing and defining success criteria, to ensure data can be recovered and transferred without disruption.

requirements.text: Firms must define the objectives of the exit strategy.
Firms must define trigger events that could activate the exit strategy, including termination of the arrangement or failure of the CSP's business activity.
Firms must perform a business impact analysis to identify resources required to implement the exit strategy.
Firms must assign roles and responsibilities to manage the exit strategy.
Firms must test the appropriateness of the exit strategy using a risk-based approach, including analyzing potential costs, impact, resources, and timing implications.
Firms must define success criteria for the transition.

sections.source_quote: 32. When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above (‘exit strategy'), the firm should consider the following: a) define the objectives of the exit strategy; b) define the trigger events that could activate the exit strategy. These should include at least the termination of the cloud outsourcing arrangement at the initiative of the firm or the CSP and the failure or other serious discontinuation of the business activity of the CSP; c) perform a business impact analysis that is commensurate to the function outsourced to identify what human and other resources would be required to implement the exit strategy; d) assign roles and responsibilities to manage the exit strategy; e) test the appropriateness of the exit strategy, using a risk-based approach, (for example, by carrying out an analysis of the potential costs, impact, resources and timing implications of transferring an outsourced service to an alternative provider); f) define success criteria of the transition.

sections.heading: A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing
sections.summary: This section requires firms to include indicators of trigger events for the exit strategy in their ongoing monitoring and oversight of the CSP's services under the cloud outsourcing arrangement.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates the inclusion of trigger event indicators in ongoing monitoring, ensuring that firms can respond promptly to changes in CSP performance or compliance.

requirements.text: Firms must include indicators of trigger events of the exit strategy in their ongoing monitoring and oversight of the CSP's services under the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.source_quote: 33. A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing monitoring and oversight of the services provided by the CSP under the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the
sections.summary: This section requires firms to ensure that the cloud outsourcing agreement does not limit their or the competent authority's ability to exercise access and audit rights and oversight options on the CSP.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that access and audit rights are not restricted in cloud outsourcing agreements, ensuring transparency and compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure that the cloud outsourcing agreement does not limit their or the competent authority's ability to exercise access and audit rights and oversight options on the CSP.

sections.source_quote: 34. A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the firm's and competent authority's effective exercise of the access and audit rights and oversight options on the CSP.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the
sections.summary: This section requires firms to ensure that the exercise of access and audit rights takes into account whether the outsourcing relates to a critical or important function and the nature and extent of the risks and impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that access and audit rights are exercised with consideration to the criticality of the function and the associated risks, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure that the exercise of access and audit rights takes into account whether the outsourcing relates to a critical or important function and the nature and extent of the risks and impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.source_quote: 35. A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the audit frequency and the areas and services to be audited) takes into consideration whether the outsourcing is related to a critical or important function, as well as the nature and extent of the risks and impact arising from the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm.

sections.heading: In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques
sections.summary: This section requires CSPs to provide a clear rationale for any risk created by the exercise of access or audit rights or the use of certain audit techniques and to agree on alternative ways to achieve similar results.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that CSPs provide rationale for risks created by audit activities and agree on alternative methods to ensure compliance without compromising service levels or data integrity.

requirements.text: CSPs must provide a clear rationale to the firm as to why the exercise of access or audit rights or the use of certain audit techniques would create a risk.
CSPs must agree with the firm on alternative ways to achieve a similar result, such as the inclusion of specific controls to be tested in a specific report/certification produced by the CSP.

sections.source_quote: 36. In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques create a risk for the environment of the CSP and/or another CSP's client (for example by impacting service levels, confidentiality, integrity and availability of data), the CSP should provide a clear rationale to the firm as to why this would create a risk and the CSP should agree with the firm on alternative ways to achieve a similar result (for example, the inclusion of specific controls to be tested in a specific report/certification produced by the CSP).

sections.heading: Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding cloud outsourcing arrangements,
sections.summary: This section allows firms to use third-party certifications and pooled audits to reduce the organisational burden on CSPs and their clients, provided they are used efficiently and effectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it allows the use of third-party certifications and pooled audits to reduce the organisational burden on CSPs and their clients, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms may use third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports made available by the CSP.
Firms may use pooled audits performed jointly with other clients of the same CSP or by a third-party auditor appointed by multiple clients of the same CSP.

sections.source_quote: 37. Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding cloud outsourcing arrangements, in order to use audit resources more efficiently and decrease the organisational burden on the CSP and its clients, firms may use: a) third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports made available by the CSP; b) pooled audits performed jointly with other clients of the same CSP or pooled audits performed by a third-party auditor appointed by multiple clients of the same CSP.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should assess whether
sections.summary: This section requires firms to assess whether third-party certifications and audit reports are adequate and sufficient to comply with their obligations under the applicable legislation and not solely rely on them over time.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that firms assess the adequacy of third-party certifications and audit reports to ensure compliance with data protection standards and not solely rely on them over time.

requirements.text: Firms must assess whether third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports are adequate and sufficient to comply with their obligations under the applicable legislation.
Firms must aim at not solely relying on these certifications and reports over time.

sections.source_quote: 38. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should assess whether the third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports referred to in paragraph 37(a) are adequate and sufficient to comply with its obligations under the applicable legislation and should aim at not solely relying on these certifications and reports over time.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should make use of the
sections.summary: This section requires firms to use third-party certifications and audit reports only if they meet specific criteria, including coverage of key systems, regular assessment of content, future coverage of key systems, satisfaction with the certifying or auditing party, adherence to appropriate standards, and contractual rights to expand the scope of certifications or audit reports.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that firms use third-party certifications and audit reports only if they meet specific criteria, ensuring compliance with data protection standards and the adequacy of audit and certification processes.

requirements.text: Firms must use third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports only if they are satisfied that the scope covers the CSP's key systems, key controls, and compliance with relevant legislation.
Firms must thoroughly assess the content of the certifications or audit reports on a regular basis and verify that they are not obsolete.
Firms must ensure that the CSP's key systems and controls are covered in future versions of the certifications or audit reports.
Firms must be satisfied with the certifying or auditing party's qualifications, expertise, re-performance/verification of the evidence, and rotation of the certifying or auditing company.
Firms must be satisfied that the certifications are issued and that the audits are performed according to appropriate standards and include a test of the effectiveness of the key controls in place.
Firms must have the contractual right to request the expansion of the scope of the certifications or audit reports to other relevant systems and controls of the CSP.
Firms must retain the contractual right to perform individual on-site audits at their discretion with regard to the outsourced function.

sections.source_quote: 39. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should make use of the third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports referred to in paragraph 37(a) only if it: a) is satisfied that the scope of the certifications or the audit reports covers the CSP's key systems (for example processes, applications, infrastructure, data centres), the key controls identified by the firm and the compliance with the relevant applicable legislation; b) thoroughly assesses the content of the certifications or audit reports on a regular basis and verify that the certifications or reports are not obsolete; c) ensures that the CSP's key systems and controls are covered in future versions of the certifications or audit reports; d) is satisfied with the certifying or auditing party (for example with regard to its qualifications, expertise, re-performance/verification of the evidence in the underlying audit file as well as rotation of the certifying or auditing company); e) is satisfied that the certifications are issued and that the audits are performed according to appropriate standards and include a test of the effectiveness of the key controls in place; f) has the contractual right to request the expansion of the scope of the certifications or audit reports to other relevant systems and controls of the CSP; the number and frequency of such requests for scope modification should be reasonable and legitimate from a risk management perspective; g) retains the contractual right to perform individual on-site audits at its discretion with regard to the outsourced function.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that, before an on-site visit, including by a third party appointed
sections.summary: This section requires firms to provide prior notice to the CSP before an on-site visit, including by a third party, unless an emergency or crisis situation makes it impractical.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that firms provide prior notice to CSPs before on-site visits, ensuring transparency and compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure that prior notice is provided to the CSP before an on-site visit, including by a third party, unless an emergency or crisis situation makes it impractical.
Such notice should include the location, purpose of the visit, and the personnel participating in the visit.

sections.source_quote: 40. A firm should ensure that, before an on-site visit, including by a third party appointed by the firm (for example an auditor), prior notice within a reasonable time period is provided to the CSP, unless an early prior notification is not possible due to an emergency or crisis situation or would lead to a situation where the audit would no longer be effective. Such notice should include the location, purpose of the visit and the personnel that will participate to the visit.

sections.heading: Considering that cloud services present a high level of technical complexity and raise
sections.summary: This section requires firms to ensure that their internal auditors and staff reviewing certifications or audit reports have the right skills and knowledge to properly assess cloud services and perform effective audits.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that firms ensure their auditors and staff have the necessary skills and knowledge to assess cloud services and audit processes, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must ensure that internal auditors and staff reviewing certifications or audit reports have the right skills and knowledge to properly assess cloud services and perform effective audits.

sections.source_quote: 41. Considering that cloud services present a high level of technical complexity and raise specific jurisdictional challenges, the staff performing the audit – being the internal auditors of the firm or auditors acting on its behalf – should have the right skills and knowledge to properly assess the relevant cloud services and perform effective and relevant audit. This should also apply to the firms' staff reviewing the certifications or audit reports provided by the CSP.

sections.heading: If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions (or material parts thereof) is
sections.summary: This section requires cloud outsourcing agreements to specify conditions for sub-outsourcing, including the CSP's accountability, notification obligations, and the firm's right to object or approve sub-outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that sub-outsourcing is subject to specific conditions, including notification, accountability, and approval rights, ensuring compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Cloud outsourcing agreements must specify any part or aspect of the outsourced function that are excluded from potential sub-outsourcing.
Cloud outsourcing agreements must indicate the conditions to be complied with in case of sub-outsourcing.
Cloud outsourcing agreements must specify that the CSP remains accountable and is obliged to oversee those services that it has sub-outsourced to ensure all contractual obligations are met.

Cloud outsourcing agreements must include an obligation for the CSP to notify the firm of any intended sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof, particularly where it might affect the CSP's ability to meet its obligations.
Cloud outsourcing agreements must allow the firm to object to the intended sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof or require explicit approval before they come into effect.

Cloud outsourcing agreements must allow the firm to terminate the arrangement if it objects to the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes and if the CSP proceeds without notification or seriously infringes the conditions.

sections.source_quote: 42. If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions (or material parts thereof) is permitted, the cloud outsourcing written agreement between the firm and the CSP should: a) specify any part or aspect of the outsourced function that are excluded from potential sub-outsourcing; b) indicate the conditions to be complied with in case of sub-outsourcing; c) specify that the CSP remains accountable and is obliged to oversee those services that it has sub-outsourced to ensure that all contractual obligations between the CSP and the firm are continuously met; d) include an obligation for the CSP to notify the firm of any intended sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, in particular where that might affect the ability of the CSP to meet its obligations under the cloud outsourcing arrangement with the firm. The notification period set in the written agreement should allow the firm sufficient time at least to carry out a risk assessment of the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof and to object to or explicitly approve them, as indicated in point (e) below; e) ensure that the firm has the right to object to the intended sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, or that explicit approval is required before the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes come into effect; f) ensure that the firm has the contractual right to terminate the cloud outsourcing arrangement with the CSP in case it objects to the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof and in case of undue sub-outsourcing (for example where the CSP proceeds with the sub-outsourcing without notifying the firm or it seriously infringes the conditions of the sub-outsourcing specified in the outsourcing agreement).

sections.heading: The firm should notify in writing its competent authority in a timely manner of planned
sections.summary: This section requires firms to notify their competent authority in writing in a timely manner of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern critical or important functions, or functions that were previously non-critical but have become critical or important.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIA as it mandates that firms notify competent authorities of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements involving critical or important functions, ensuring transparency and compliance with data protection standards.

requirements.text: Firms must notify their competent authority in writing in a timely manner of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern critical or important functions.

Firms must also notify their competent authority in writing in a timely manner of cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern functions previously classified as non-critical or non-important but have become critical or important.

sections.source_quote: 44. The firm should notify in writing its competent authority in a timely manner of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern a critical or important function. The firm should also notify in a timely manner and in writing its competent authority of those cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern a function that was previously classified as non-critical or non-important and then became critical or important.

sections.heading: The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of
sections.summary: Section 45 requires firms to provide detailed written notifications when outsourcing cloud services. The notification must include specific information about the outsourcing agreement, the CSP, the data stored, and the risk assessments conducted. This ensures transparency and allows regulators to assess the risks involved.
sections.dtia_summary: Section 45 is critical for DTIAs as it mandates detailed documentation of cloud outsourcing arrangements, including data storage locations and risk assessments, which are essential for evaluating cross-border data transfers and compliance with GDPR requirements.

requirements.text: The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of proportionality, at least the following information: the start date of the cloud outsourcing agreement and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm.
A brief description of the outsourced function.
A brief summary of the reasons why the outsourced function is considered critical or important.

The name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier (where available), its registered address, its relevant contact details, and the name of its parent company (if any).
The governing law of the cloud outsourcing agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction.
The cloud deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held by the CSP and the locations (namely regions or countries) where such data will be stored.
The date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function.
The date of the most recent risk assessment or audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment or audit.
The individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

Where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which material parts of a critical or important function are sub-outsourced, including the country or region where the sub-outsourcers are registered, where the sub-outsourced service will be performed, and where the data will be stored.

sections.source_quote: The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of proportionality, at least the following information: the start date of the cloud outsourcing agreement and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm;

sections.heading: Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: Section 46 mandates that competent authorities assess the risks associated with firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements, particularly those involving the outsourcing of critical or important functions. This ensures that regulatory oversight is maintained and potential risks are identified.
sections.dtia_summary: Section 46 is relevant to DTIAs as it requires competent authorities to evaluate the risks of cloud outsourcing, especially for critical functions, which impacts the assessment of cross-border data transfers and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements as part of their supervisory process. In particular, this assessment should focus on the arrangements that relate to the outsourcing of critical or important functions.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements as part of their supervisory process.

sections.heading: Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective
sections.summary: Section 47 requires competent authorities to ensure they can perform effective supervision, especially when firms outsource critical or important functions outside the EU. This ensures that regulatory oversight remains robust and that potential risks are managed.
sections.dtia_summary: Section 47 is relevant to DTIAs as it emphasizes the need for competent authorities to maintain effective supervision of cloud outsourcing, particularly when functions are outsourced outside the EU, which affects cross-border data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective supervision, in particular when firms outsource critical or important functions that are performed outside the EU.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective supervision, in particular when firms outsource critical or important functions that are performed outside the EU.

sections.heading: Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms:
sections.summary: Section 48 requires competent authorities to assess whether firms have appropriate governance, resources, and processes in place to manage cloud outsourcing arrangements. This includes identifying and managing all relevant risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Section 48 is relevant to DTIAs as it mandates that competent authorities evaluate firms' governance and risk management practices for cloud outsourcing, which is essential for assessing the security and compliance of cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms: have in place the relevant governance, resources and operational processes to appropriately and effectively enter into, implement, and oversee cloud outsourcing arrangements.
Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms: identify and manage all relevant risks related to cloud outsourcing.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms: have in place the relevant governance, resources and operational processes to appropriately and effectively enter into, implement, and oversee cloud outsourcing arrangements;

sections.heading: Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the
sections.summary: Section 49 requires competent authorities to monitor and evaluate concentration risks associated with cloud outsourcing, particularly their potential impact on other firms and the stability of the financial market.
sections.dtia_summary: Section 49 is relevant to DTIAs as it mandates monitoring of concentration risks in cloud outsourcing, which affects the assessment of cross-border data transfers and the stability of financial systems.

requirements.text: Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the development of such risks and evaluate both their potential impact on other firms they supervise and the stability of the financial market.

sections.source_quote: Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the development of such risks and evaluate both their potential impact on other firms they supervise and the stability of the financial market.
